1:
"And after these things I heard a great voice of much people in heaven, saying, Alleluia; Salvation, and glory, and honour, and power, unto the Lord our God:" The witnesses note heaven's Alleluia at Babylon's fall. On "I heard a great voice of much people in heaven, saying, Alleluia," the grammatical voices note the heavenly praise. Barnes and Robertson note the response to Babylon's fall: "a great voice of much people in heaven, saying, Alleluia; Salvation, and glory, and honour, and power, unto the Lord our God" — in answer to the call to rejoice (18:20), a great multitude in heaven cries "Alleluia" (Hallelujah, "praise the LORD" — the first of four in this chapter, the only occurrences in the NT); they ascribe salvation, glory, honor, and power to God for his judgment on Babylon.

On "Alleluia," the witnesses note the praise. Barnes and Henry note that "Alleluia" (the Hebrew shout of praise from the Psalms) opens the heavenly celebration; the redeemed and heavenly host break into praise at God's righteous judgment.

On "Salvation, and glory, and honour, and power, unto the Lord our God," the latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede and the grammatical-historical note the doxology: heaven ascribes "salvation, and glory, and honour, and power" to God — the fourfold doxology magnifying God as the author of salvation and the sovereign whose judgments are glorious; Victorinus and Bede read the heavenly rejoicing of the church triumphant over the fall of the great city. The verse displays heaven's Alleluia at Babylon's fall.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the praise — "a great voice of much people in heaven, saying, Alleluia; Salvation, and glory, and honour, and power, unto the Lord our God," heaven's praise at God's judgment on Babylon (18:20, Barnes, Robertson); "Alleluia" the Hebrew shout of praise opening the celebration (Barnes, Henry); the fourfold doxology magnifying God (Victorinus, Bede). The verse displays heaven's Alleluia. The verse marks the voice: a great multitude. The verse displays the cry: Alleluia. The verse displays the doxology: salvation, glory, honour, power. The verse displays the ground: God's judgment. The praise displays heaven rejoicing at God's righteous judgment. The verse displays the multitude: praising. The verse displays the Alleluia: sung. The verse displays the God: glorified. Heaven's Alleluia at Babylon's fall displays the heavenly celebration: a great multitude in heaven cries "Alleluia; salvation, and glory, and honour, and power, unto the Lord our God" — heaven rejoicing and ascribing all praise to God for his righteous judgment on Babylon. The verse displays the heavenly praise, for God's true judgments (v.2).
2:
"For true and righteous are his judgments: for he hath judged the great whore, which did corrupt the earth with her fornication, and hath avenged the blood of his servants at her hand." The brief verse notes the praise of God's true judgment on the whore. The grammatical voices note the vindication. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of the Alleluia: "true and righteous are his judgments: for he hath judged the great whore... and hath avenged the blood of his servants at her hand" — heaven praises God because his judgments are true and righteous; he has judged the great whore (Babylon) who corrupted the earth, and avenged the blood of his servants; the judgment vindicates God's justice and his people.

On "true and righteous are his judgments," the witnesses note the vindication of God's justice. Barnes and JFB note that "true and righteous are his judgments" (as 15:3; 16:7) — God's judgments are faithful and just; heaven affirms the rightness of the judgment on Babylon.

On "he hath judged the great whore... and hath avenged the blood of his servants at her hand," the grammatical-historical note the twofold ground: God "hath judged the great whore, which did corrupt the earth with her fornication, and hath avenged the blood of his servants at her hand" — he judged her for corrupting the earth and avenged the blood of his martyred servants (18:24; 6:10); the two grounds — her corruption and her bloodguilt — justify the praise; God has answered the martyrs' cry. The verse displays the praise of God's true judgment on the whore.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vindication — "true and righteous are his judgments: for he hath judged the great whore... and hath avenged the blood of his servants," God's just judgment on Babylon, vindicating his people (Barnes, Robertson); "true and righteous are his judgments" (15:3; 16:7, Barnes, JFB); "he hath judged the great whore... avenged the blood of his servants," her corruption and bloodguilt the grounds, the martyrs' cry answered (18:24; 6:10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vindicated judgment. The verse marks the judgments: true and righteous. The verse displays the whore: judged. The verse displays the corruption: of the earth. The verse displays the blood: avenged. The praise displays God's just judgment vindicating his people. The verse displays the judgment: righteous. The verse displays the whore: punished. The verse displays the servants: avenged. The praise of God's true judgment on the whore displays the ground of heaven's Alleluia: true and righteous are his judgments, for he has judged the great whore who corrupted the earth and avenged the blood of his servants — God's justice vindicated and his martyred people avenged. The verse displays the vindicated judgment, with the second Alleluia (v.3).
3:
"And again they said, Alleluia. And her smoke rose up for ever and ever." The very brief verse notes the second Alleluia and the rising smoke of Babylon. The grammatical voices note the renewed praise. Barnes and Robertson note the second shout: "again they said, Alleluia. And her smoke rose up for ever and ever" — heaven utters a second "Alleluia," and the smoke of Babylon's burning rises forever; the praise is renewed, and the perpetual smoke marks the permanence of her judgment.

On "again they said, Alleluia," the witnesses note the repeated praise. Barnes and Gill note that the second "Alleluia" intensifies the heavenly celebration; the praise of God for his judgment is repeated and emphatic.

On "her smoke rose up for ever and ever," the grammatical-historical note the perpetual judgment: "her smoke rose up for ever and ever" — the smoke of her burning ascends forever (drawn from Isaiah 34:10, the perpetual desolation of Edom); the figure of the permanence and finality of her ruin; the rising smoke is the eternal token of her judgment, occasioning unending praise. The verse displays the second Alleluia and the rising smoke of Babylon.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the renewed praise — "again they said, Alleluia. And her smoke rose up for ever and ever," the second Alleluia and the perpetual smoke of her judgment (Barnes, Robertson); the repeated "Alleluia" intensifying the celebration (Barnes, Gill); "her smoke rose up for ever and ever," the permanence of her ruin (Isaiah 34:10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renewed praise. The verse marks the cry: again, Alleluia. The verse displays the smoke: rising forever. The verse displays the judgment: perpetual. The verse displays the praise: intensified. The shout displays heaven's renewed praise at the permanent judgment. The verse displays the Alleluia: repeated. The verse displays the smoke: eternal. The verse displays the ruin: final. The second Alleluia and the rising smoke of Babylon display the renewed heavenly praise: again they cry "Alleluia," and her smoke rises forever and ever — the perpetual token of her final judgment, occasioning unending praise. The verse displays the rising smoke, before the elders' worship (v.4).
4:
"And the four and twenty elders and the four beasts fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne, saying, Amen; Alleluia." The brief verse notes the elders and living creatures worshipping with Amen and Alleluia. The grammatical voices note the heavenly worship. Barnes and Robertson note the adoration: "the four and twenty elders and the four beasts (living creatures) fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne, saying, Amen; Alleluia" — the twenty-four elders (the redeemed church) and the four living creatures fall down and worship God on the throne, sealing the heavenly praise with "Amen; Alleluia"; the highest heavenly beings join and confirm the celebration.

On "fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne," the witnesses note the adoration. Barnes and Ellicott note that the elders and living creatures "fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne" — the representatives of the redeemed and the heavenly host bow in worship; the adoration of God for his judgment.

On "saying, Amen; Alleluia," the grammatical-historical note the confirming praise: "Amen; Alleluia" — they confirm ("Amen," so be it, it is true) and praise ("Alleluia," the third in the chapter) God's judgment; the elders and creatures seal the heavenly Alleluia with their assent and adoration. The verse displays the elders and living creatures worshipping with Amen and Alleluia.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the worship — "the four and twenty elders and the four beasts fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne, saying, Amen; Alleluia," the elders and living creatures worshiping and sealing the praise (Barnes, Robertson); "fell down and worshipped God that sat on the throne," the adoration of the redeemed and heavenly host (Barnes, Ellicott); "Amen; Alleluia," their confirming praise (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the worshipping elders. The verse marks the worshipers: elders and creatures. The verse displays the posture: fell down. The verse displays the worship: of God on the throne. The verse displays the praise: Amen, Alleluia. The adoration displays heaven sealing the celebration of God's judgment. The verse displays the elders: bowing. The verse displays the creatures: worshiping. The verse displays the Amen: confirming. The elders and living creatures worshipping with Amen and Alleluia display the heavenly worship that seals the praise: the twenty-four elders and the four living creatures fall down and worship God on the throne, saying "Amen; Alleluia" — confirming and adoring God for his righteous judgment. The verse displays the worshipping host, before the call to praise (v.5).
5:
"And a voice came out of the throne, saying, Praise our God, all ye his servants, and ye that fear him, both small and great." The brief verse notes the call from the throne to praise God. The grammatical voices note the summons. Barnes and Robertson note the call: "a voice came out of the throne, saying, Praise our God, all ye his servants, and ye that fear him, both small and great" — a voice from the throne (perhaps an angel or one of the living creatures) calls all God's servants to praise him; the summons to universal praise prepares for the great Alleluia of the marriage (vv.6-7).

On "Praise our God, all ye his servants, and ye that fear him," the witnesses note the called worshipers. Barnes and Henry note that "all ye his servants, and ye that fear him" — all who serve and fear God are called to praise; the summons embraces the whole company of the faithful.

On "both small and great," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the universal scope: "both small and great" — all the faithful, of every rank and degree, are called to praise; none is excluded; the great and the lowly alike join in the praise of God; the comprehensive summons to worship. The verse displays the call from the throne to praise God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "a voice came out of the throne, saying, Praise our God, all ye his servants, and ye that fear him, both small and great," the call from the throne to universal praise (Barnes, Robertson); "all ye his servants, and ye that fear him," all the faithful called to praise (Barnes, Henry); "both small and great," the universal scope, all ranks included (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the call to praise. The verse marks the voice: from the throne. The verse displays the call: praise our God. The verse displays the called: his servants. The verse displays the scope: small and great. The summons displays all the faithful called to praise God. The verse displays the voice: calling. The verse displays the servants: summoned. The verse displays the praise: universal. The call from the throne to praise God displays the summons that opens the marriage-celebration: a voice from the throne calls all God's servants who fear him, small and great, to praise him — the universal summons to worship. The verse displays the call to praise, answered by the great Alleluia (v.6).
6:
"And I heard as it were the voice of a great multitude, and as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of mighty thunderings, saying, Alleluia: for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth." The witnesses note the great Alleluia for God's reign. On "the voice of a great multitude, and as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of mighty thunderings, saying, Alleluia," the grammatical voices note the mighty praise. Barnes and Robertson note the climactic shout: "the voice of a great multitude, and as... many waters, and... mighty thunderings, saying, Alleluia" — a great multitude, like the roar of many waters and mighty thunder, cries "Alleluia" (the fourth and climactic); the overwhelming, majestic praise of the whole heavenly host.

On "the voice of many waters... mighty thunderings," the witnesses note the majesty. Barnes and Gill note that the praise, "as the voice of many waters and mighty thunderings" (cf. 14:2), marks the immense volume and power of the heavenly chorus; the praise is mighty as the sea and the storm.

On "for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth," the grammatical-historical note the ground: "for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth" — the climactic Alleluia is grounded in God's reign; the Almighty has taken his power and reigns (11:17); the establishment of God's unhindered, triumphant kingdom (after the fall of Babylon) is the cause of the great praise. The verse displays the great Alleluia for God's reign.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mighty praise — "the voice of a great multitude, and as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of mighty thunderings, saying, Alleluia," the climactic, majestic praise of the heavenly host (14:2, Barnes, Robertson); "as the voice of many waters... mighty thunderings," the immense volume and power of the chorus (Barnes, Gill); "for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth," God's triumphant reign the ground of the praise (11:17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the great Alleluia. The verse marks the voice: a great multitude. The verse displays the majesty: waters and thunder. The verse displays the cry: Alleluia. The verse displays the ground: God reigns. The praise displays heaven's mighty Alleluia at God's reign. The verse displays the multitude: thundering. The verse displays the Alleluia: climactic. The verse displays the reign: God's. The great Alleluia for God's reign displays the climax of the heavenly praise: a great multitude, like many waters and mighty thunder, cries "Alleluia, for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth" — heaven's mighty celebration of God's triumphant, established reign. The verse displays the reigning God, ushering in the marriage of the Lamb (v.7).
7:
"Let us be glad and rejoice, and give honour to him: for the marriage of the Lamb is come, and his wife hath made herself ready." The witnesses note the marriage of the Lamb and the ready bride. On "Let us be glad and rejoice, and give honour to him: for the marriage of the Lamb is come," the grammatical voices note the joyful announcement. Barnes and Robertson note the cause of joy: "Let us be glad and rejoice, and give honour to him: for the marriage of the Lamb is come" — the heavenly host rejoices because the marriage of the Lamb has come; the long-awaited union of Christ (the Lamb) with his bride (the church) is at hand; the figure of the consummated, glorious union of Christ and his people.

On "the marriage of the Lamb," the witnesses note the union of Christ and the church. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the marriage of the Lamb" is the consummation of the union between Christ and the church (the bride, 21:2, 9; cf. Ephesians 5:25-32; Matthew 22:2); the figure of the final, blessed union of the redeemed with their Lord; the cause of heaven's joy.

On "his wife hath made herself ready," the grammatical-historical note the prepared bride: "his wife (bride) hath made herself ready" — the bride (the church) has prepared herself for the marriage; her readiness (her purity and adornment, v.8) is the fruit of grace; the church is made ready for her Lord. The verse displays the marriage of the Lamb and the ready bride.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the marriage — "Let us be glad and rejoice... for the marriage of the Lamb is come, and his wife hath made herself ready," heaven rejoicing at the union of the Lamb and his bride (Barnes, Robertson); "the marriage of the Lamb" the consummated union of Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:25-32, Barnes, Ellicott); "his wife hath made herself ready," the prepared, adorned bride (v.8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Lamb's marriage. The verse marks the joy: be glad and rejoice. The verse displays the marriage: of the Lamb. The verse displays the bride: his wife. The verse displays the readiness: made herself ready. The announcement displays the joyful union of Christ and the church. The verse displays the marriage: come. The verse displays the bride: ready. The verse displays the joy: heaven's. The marriage of the Lamb and the ready bride display the cause of heaven's joy: let us be glad and give honour to him, for the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his bride has made herself ready — the consummated, glorious union of Christ and his church. The verse displays the ready bride, clothed in fine linen (v.8).
8:
"And to her was granted that she should be arrayed in fine linen, clean and white: for the fine linen is the righteousness of saints." The witnesses note the bride's fine linen, the righteousness of saints. On "to her was granted that she should be arrayed in fine linen, clean and white," the grammatical voices note the bride's garment. Barnes and Robertson note the array: "to her was granted that she should be arrayed in fine linen, clean and white" — the bride is granted (it is given to her — by grace) to be clothed in fine linen, clean and white; the pure white garment is the bride's adornment, in pointed contrast to the harlot's purple and scarlet (17:4; 18:16); the church's purity is a gift of grace ("granted").

On "clean and white," the witnesses note the purity. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "fine linen, clean and white (bright)" marks the purity, holiness, and righteousness of the bride; the spotless garment of the church made ready for her Lord.

On "the fine linen is the righteousness of saints," the Reformed Gill and grammatical-historical note the interpretation: "the fine linen is the righteousness (righteous acts, dikaiomata) of saints" — the white linen is explained as the righteousness of the saints; whether their righteous deeds (the fruit of grace, the visible holiness of the redeemed) or, more deeply, the righteousness of Christ imputed and worked in them; the bride's garment is the righteousness in which the saints are arrayed, granted by grace. The verse displays the bride's fine linen, the righteousness of saints.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the garment — "to her was granted that she should be arrayed in fine linen, clean and white," the bride clothed by grace in pure white linen, contrasting the harlot (17:4, Barnes, Robertson); "clean and white" the purity and holiness of the bride (Barnes, Ellicott); "the fine linen is the righteousness of saints," the white linen the righteousness (righteous deeds, or Christ's righteousness) of the saints (Gill, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bride's garment. The verse marks the linen: fine, granted. The verse displays the purity: clean and white. The verse displays the meaning: the righteousness of saints. The verse displays the contrast: with the harlot. The garment displays the bride arrayed in righteousness by grace. The verse displays the linen: white. The verse displays the righteousness: of saints. The verse displays the grant: of grace. The bride's fine linen, the righteousness of saints, displays the church's pure adornment: it is granted her to be arrayed in fine linen, clean and white — the fine linen being the righteousness of the saints; the bride clothed, by grace, in the spotless garment of righteousness. The verse displays the arrayed bride, with the beatitude on those called to the marriage supper (v.9).
9:
"And he saith unto me, Write, Blessed are they which are called unto the marriage supper of the Lamb. And he saith unto me, These are the true sayings of God." The witnesses note the beatitude on those called to the marriage supper. On "Write, Blessed are they which are called unto the marriage supper of the Lamb," the grammatical voices note the beatitude. Barnes and Robertson note the blessing: "Write, Blessed are they which are called unto the marriage supper of the Lamb" — the angel bids John write a beatitude: blessed are those invited to the marriage supper of the Lamb; the figure of the great feast of the consummated union (cf. Matthew 22:2-3; Luke 14:15-16); to be called (invited and admitted) to the Lamb's marriage feast is blessedness.

On "the marriage supper of the Lamb," the witnesses note the feast. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the marriage supper of the Lamb" is the joyful feast celebrating the union of Christ and the church; those called to it are the redeemed who share the blessing of the consummation; the guests (and the bride) are the saved.

On "These are the true sayings of God," the grammatical-historical note the confirmation: "These are the true (genuine) sayings of God" — the angel affirms that these words (the beatitude, and the visions) are the true, faithful words of God; the assurance that what is promised is sure; God's word is true and will be fulfilled. The verse displays the beatitude on those called to the marriage supper.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the beatitude — "Write, Blessed are they which are called unto the marriage supper of the Lamb," the blessing on those invited to the Lamb's feast (Matthew 22:2, Barnes, Robertson); "the marriage supper of the Lamb" the joyful feast of the consummated union, the redeemed its guests (Barnes, Ellicott); "These are the true sayings of God," the assurance that the words are God's true and sure word (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the marriage-supper beatitude. The verse marks the blessing: blessed are the called. The verse displays the feast: the marriage supper. The verse displays the guests: the redeemed. The verse displays the assurance: true sayings of God. The beatitude displays the blessedness of those at the Lamb's feast. The verse displays the called: blessed. The verse displays the supper: of the Lamb. The verse displays the word: true. The beatitude on those called to the marriage supper displays the blessing of the consummation: blessed are they who are called to the marriage supper of the Lamb — and these are the true sayings of God; the redeemed admitted to the joyful feast of the Lamb's union with his church. The verse displays the blessed guests, occasioning John's mistaken worship (v.10).
10:
"And I fell at his feet to worship him. And he said unto me, See thou do it not: I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren that have the testimony of Jesus: worship God: for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy." The witnesses note John forbidden to worship the angel, with the testimony of Jesus the spirit of prophecy. On "I fell at his feet to worship him. And he said unto me, See thou do it not," the grammatical voices note the refused worship. Barnes and Robertson note John's error and the angel's refusal: "I fell at his feet to worship him. And he said unto me, See thou do it not" — overwhelmed, John falls to worship the angel, but the angel forbids it ("See thou do it not"); the angel refuses the worship that belongs to God alone (cf. 22:8-9).

On "I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren that have the testimony of Jesus: worship God," the witnesses note the angel's humility. Barnes and Ellicott note the reason: "I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren that have the testimony of Jesus: worship God" — the angel is a fellow servant of God with John and the brethren who hold the testimony of Jesus; he directs the worship to God alone; angels are not to be worshiped (a rebuke to angel-worship).

On "for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy," the grammatical-historical note the famous saying: "the testimony of Jesus is the spirit (substance, animating principle) of prophecy" — the witness to Jesus is the heart and soul of all prophecy; whether "the testimony borne by Jesus" or "the testimony concerning/about Jesus," the meaning is that the spirit and substance of prophecy is to testify of Christ; all true prophecy points to and bears witness of Jesus; this binds the prophet, the angel, and John as fellow servants in the one testimony. The verse displays John forbidden to worship the angel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the refusal — "I fell at his feet to worship him. And he said unto me, See thou do it not," John's mistaken worship refused by the angel (22:8-9, Barnes, Robertson); "I am thy fellowservant... worship God," the angel a fellow servant, directing worship to God alone (Barnes, Ellicott); "the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy," the witness to Jesus the heart and substance of all prophecy (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the refused worship. The verse marks the worship: at the angel's feet. The verse displays the refusal: do it not. The verse displays the angel: a fellowservant. The verse displays the saying: testimony of Jesus, spirit of prophecy. The rebuke displays worship reserved for God and prophecy centered on Christ. The verse displays the worship: forbidden. The verse displays the angel: humble. The verse displays the testimony: of Jesus. John forbidden to worship the angel, with the testimony of Jesus the spirit of prophecy, displays the rebuke of angel-worship and the heart of prophecy: the angel refuses John's worship as a fellow servant and bids him worship God, for the testimony of Jesus is the spirit of prophecy. The verse displays the corrected John, before the rider on the white horse (v.11).
11:
"And I saw heaven opened, and behold a white horse; and he that sat upon him was called Faithful and True, and in righteousness he doth judge and make war." The witnesses note the rider on the white horse, Faithful and True. On "I saw heaven opened, and behold a white horse; and he that sat upon him was called Faithful and True," the grammatical voices note the appearance of Christ. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "heaven opened, and behold a white horse; and he that sat upon him was called Faithful and True" — heaven opens and reveals a rider on a white horse, called "Faithful and True"; the rider is Christ (the Word of God, KING OF KINGS, vv.13, 16), coming forth in triumph; the white horse is the emblem of victory and conquest (6:2); his title "Faithful and True" (3:14) marks his trustworthiness and truth.

On the nature of the rider's coming, interpreters note the frame. The grammatical-historical Barnes and the futurist read the rider's going forth as Christ's coming to judge and conquer his enemies — many take it of the second advent of Christ in glory to the final battle and judgment; the Reformed Gill reads it of Christ coming forth in the latter days, through the progress of the gospel and the overthrow of antichrist, "Christ to the last judgment"; some read the conquest as the triumphant advance of the Word/gospel; the literal-future and the gospel-progress readings differ on the mode of the coming.

On "in righteousness he doth judge and make war," the grammatical-historical note his righteous war: "in righteousness he doth judge and make war" — unlike earthly conquerors, Christ judges and wages war in righteousness; all his acts of judgment and conquest are just; he comes to judge and to war against the wicked in perfect righteousness. The verse displays the rider on the white horse, Faithful and True.

The witnesses agree the substance: heaven opens and Christ appears as a rider on a white horse, called Faithful and True, judging and warring in righteousness (Barnes, Robertson, Gill). The nature of his coming bears a frame-difference: the literal second advent to the final battle and judgment (futurist/grammatical-historical) vs the conquering progress of Christ and his gospel through the latter days against antichrist (Gill, historicist). The verse displays the rider crux. The verse marks the heaven: opened. The verse displays the horse: white. The verse displays the rider: Faithful and True. The verse displays the war: in righteousness. The vision displays Christ coming forth to righteous conquest. The verse displays the rider: revealed. The verse displays the title: Faithful and True. The verse displays the judgment: righteous. The rider on the white horse, Faithful and True, displays the appearance of the conquering Christ: heaven opens and reveals the rider called Faithful and True, who judges and makes war in righteousness — read by some as the literal second advent to judgment, by others as Christ's triumphant advance through the gospel against antichrist. The verse displays the conquering Christ, his eyes of fire and many crowns (v.12).
12:
"His eyes were as a flame of fire, and on his head were many crowns; and he had a name written, that no man knew, but he himself." The brief verse notes the rider's flaming eyes, many crowns, and secret name. The grammatical voices note the description of Christ. Barnes and Robertson note the features: "his eyes were as a flame of fire, and on his head were many crowns; and he had a name written, that no man knew, but he himself" — the rider (Christ) has eyes like a flame of fire (1:14; 2:18, his penetrating, all-searching gaze), many crowns (diadems) on his head (marking his supreme, manifold sovereignty), and a secret name known only to himself.

On "his eyes were as a flame of fire, and on his head were many crowns," the witnesses note his majesty. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "eyes as a flame of fire" signify his searching omniscience, and the "many crowns (diadems)" his royal supremacy over all — many crowns, for he is King of kings (v.16); the conqueror crowned with manifold sovereignty.

On "a name written, that no man knew, but he himself," the grammatical-historical note the secret name: "a name written, that no man knew, but he himself" — Christ has a name unknown to all but himself; the figure of his incomprehensible, transcendent nature; there is in Christ a depth of being and dignity beyond full human knowledge; the secret name marks his unsearchable majesty. The verse displays the rider's flaming eyes, many crowns, and secret name.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the features — "his eyes were as a flame of fire, and on his head were many crowns; and he had a name written, that no man knew," Christ's searching eyes, manifold crowns, and secret name (1:14; 2:18, Barnes, Robertson); "eyes as a flame of fire... many crowns" his omniscience and royal supremacy (Barnes, Ellicott); "a name... that no man knew, but he himself," his incomprehensible, transcendent nature (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the majestic rider. The verse marks the eyes: flame of fire. The verse displays the crowns: many. The verse displays the name: secret. The verse displays the majesty: unsearchable. The description displays Christ omniscient, sovereign, and transcendent. The verse displays the eyes: searching. The verse displays the crowns: manifold. The verse displays the name: hidden. The rider's flaming eyes, many crowns, and secret name display the majesty of the conquering Christ: his eyes are a flame of fire, his head bears many crowns, and he has a name known only to himself — the all-searching, supremely sovereign, transcendent Lord. The verse displays the majestic rider, clothed in a vesture dipped in blood (v.13).
13:
"And he was clothed with a vesture dipped in blood: and his name is called The Word of God." The witnesses note the vesture dipped in blood and the name the Word of God. On "he was clothed with a vesture dipped in blood," interpreters divide over the blood. The grammatical voices note the bloodstained robe. The Reformed Gill notes both readings: "clothed with a vesture dipped in blood — either in his own (the blood of his atonement), or rather in the blood of his enemies, with which he appears stained" — the dominant reading (Gill, Robertson, Wesley) takes it as the blood of his enemies, slain in judgment (Isaiah 63:1-3, the treading of the winepress, his garments stained red); a second reading takes it as Christ's own blood, the blood of the cross/atonement. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson favor the blood of his enemies (the conqueror's robe stained with the blood of those he treads down), though noting the alternative.

On "a vesture dipped in blood," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Ellicott note that the robe "dipped in blood" marks him as a victorious warrior — the blood being that of his enemies (Isaiah 63), the sign of his conquest and judgment; the conqueror returning from the slaughter of his foes, his garments red.

On "his name is called The Word of God," the grammatical-historical note the divine title: "his name is called The Word of God (the Logos)" — the rider is the Word of God (John 1:1, 14; 1 John 1:1); the title affirms his deity and his role as the revealer and executor of God's will; the eternal Word, now coming in judgment. The verse displays the vesture dipped in blood and the name the Word of God.

The witnesses agree the substance: the rider wears a blood-dipped robe and is named the Word of God (Gill, Barnes, Robertson). The blood divides interpreters: the blood of his enemies, slain in judgment (the dominant reading, Isaiah 63:1-3 — Gill, Robertson, Wesley) vs his own atoning blood of the cross. The verse displays the blood crux. The verse marks the vesture: dipped in blood. The verse displays the debate: enemies' blood or his own. The verse displays the name: the Word of God. The verse displays the deity: the Logos. The figure displays the conquering Word in his bloodstained robe. The verse displays the robe: bloodied. The verse displays the Word: of God. The verse displays the conqueror: stained. The vesture dipped in blood and the name the Word of God display the conquering Christ: clothed in a robe dipped in blood — read chiefly as the blood of his enemies slain in judgment (Isaiah 63:1-3), and by some as his own atoning blood — and named the Word of God, the eternal Logos coming in judgment. The verse displays the Word of God, leading the armies of heaven (v.14).
14:
"And the armies which were in heaven followed him upon white horses, clothed in fine linen, white and clean." The brief verse notes the armies of heaven following the rider. The grammatical voices note the heavenly host. Barnes and Robertson note the following armies: "the armies which were in heaven followed him upon white horses, clothed in fine linen, white and clean" — the heavenly armies follow Christ, mounted on white horses and clothed in fine white linen; the host accompanying the conquering Christ; whether angels, or the redeemed saints (the bride's array, v.8), or both, follow him to the battle.

On "upon white horses," the witnesses note their victory-array. Barnes and Ellicott note that the armies ride "upon white horses" — sharing the white horse of the conqueror (v.11); the white marks victory and purity; the host triumphs with Christ.

On "clothed in fine linen, white and clean," the grammatical-historical note their pure garments: "clothed in fine linen, white and clean" — the same fine linen as the bride (v.8, the righteousness of saints); the armies are arrayed in purity and righteousness; whether the saints (in their righteousness) or the holy angels; they follow the Word of God in spotless white, not to fight (the victory is Christ's alone, v.15), but to share his triumph. The verse displays the armies of heaven following the rider.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the host — "the armies which were in heaven followed him upon white horses, clothed in fine linen, white and clean," the heavenly armies following Christ in white (Barnes, Robertson); "upon white horses" sharing the conqueror's victory-array (v.11, Barnes, Ellicott); "clothed in fine linen, white and clean," the pure garments of the saints/angels, the righteousness of the redeemed (v.8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the following armies. The verse marks the armies: of heaven. The verse displays the horses: white. The verse displays the linen: white and clean. The verse displays the host: following Christ. The vision displays the heavenly host accompanying the conqueror. The verse displays the armies: following. The verse displays the white: pure. The verse displays the host: triumphant. The armies of heaven following the rider display the host accompanying Christ: the armies of heaven follow him on white horses, clothed in fine white linen — the saints and angels, arrayed in purity, sharing the triumph of the conquering Word. The verse displays the heavenly armies, while Christ smites the nations (v.15).
15:
"And out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations: and he shall rule them with a rod of iron: and he treadeth the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God." The witnesses note the sword of his mouth and the winepress of wrath. On "out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations," the grammatical voices note the word of judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the sword: "out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations" — from Christ's mouth proceeds a sharp sword (the word of his judgment, 1:16; 2:12; Isaiah 11:4; 2 Thessalonians 2:8), with which he smites the nations; he conquers not by carnal weapons but by the word of his power; his judgment goes forth from his mouth.

On "he shall rule them with a rod of iron," the witnesses note his sovereign rule. Barnes and Ellicott note that "he shall rule (shepherd) them with a rod of iron" (Psalm 2:9; 12:5) — Christ rules the nations with firm, irresistible authority; the rod of iron marks his unbreakable sovereignty over the nations he conquers.

On "he treadeth the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the winepress: "he treadeth the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God" — Christ treads the winepress of God's fierce wrath (14:19-20; Isaiah 63:3); he executes the judgment of God on the wicked, treading them as grapes in the winepress; the conquering Christ is the executor of the Almighty's wrath. The verse displays the sword of his mouth and the winepress of wrath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sword — "out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations," the word of judgment by which Christ smites the nations (1:16; Isaiah 11:4; 2 Thessalonians 2:8, Barnes, Robertson); "he shall rule them with a rod of iron," his firm, sovereign rule (Psalm 2:9, Barnes, Ellicott); "he treadeth the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God," Christ executing God's fierce wrath on the wicked (14:19-20; Isaiah 63:3, Cyril). The verse displays the judging Christ. The verse marks the sword: from his mouth. The verse displays the smiting: of the nations. The verse displays the rule: rod of iron. The verse displays the winepress: of wrath. The judgment displays Christ smiting and treading the nations by God's wrath. The verse displays the sword: sharp. The verse displays the rod: of iron. The verse displays the winepress: trodden. The sword of his mouth and the winepress of wrath display the conquering judgment of Christ: from his mouth goes a sharp sword to smite the nations, he rules them with a rod of iron, and he treads the winepress of the fierce wrath of Almighty God — the Word executing the judgment of God on the wicked. The verse displays the judging Christ, named King of kings (v.16).
16:
"And he hath on his vesture and on his thigh a name written, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS." The witnesses note the rider's name, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS. On "he hath on his vesture and on his thigh a name written," the grammatical voices note the inscribed title. Barnes and Robertson note the name: "on his vesture and on his thigh a name written, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS" — the rider bears, on his robe and on his thigh (where the sword hangs, or where the inscription would be visible), the title "KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS"; the open proclamation of his supreme sovereignty.

On "on his vesture and on his thigh," the witnesses note the placement. Barnes and Gill note that the name is on "his vesture and on his thigh" — conspicuously displayed, where all may see; the title is publicly inscribed on the conqueror; (the thigh, where the warrior's sword is girt, or the prominent place of the inscription).

On "KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the supreme title: "KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS" — the title of Christ's absolute supremacy (17:14; 1 Timothy 6:15; Deuteronomy 10:17); he is sovereign over all kings and lords, the supreme Ruler of all; the conquering Christ is openly proclaimed as the universal King and Lord. The verse displays the rider's name, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the title — "on his vesture and on his thigh a name written, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS," the conqueror's name of supreme sovereignty (17:14, Barnes, Robertson); "on his vesture and on his thigh," the conspicuous, public placement (Barnes, Gill); "KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS," Christ's absolute supremacy over all (1 Timothy 6:15, Cyril). The verse displays the supreme title. The verse marks the name: on his vesture and thigh. The verse displays the placement: conspicuous. The verse displays the title: King of kings. The verse displays the sovereignty: supreme. The name displays Christ openly proclaimed as universal King and Lord. The verse displays the name: inscribed. The verse displays the King: of kings. The verse displays the Lord: of lords. The rider's name, KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS, displays the open proclamation of Christ's supremacy: on his robe and thigh is written "KING OF KINGS, AND LORD OF LORDS" — the conquering Christ proclaimed as the supreme Ruler over all kings and lords. The verse displays the supreme King, before the calling of the birds to the great supper (v.17).
17:
"And I saw an angel standing in the sun; and he cried with a loud voice, saying to all the fowls that fly in the midst of heaven, Come and gather yourselves together unto the supper of the great God;" The brief verse notes the angel calling the birds to the great supper of God. The grammatical voices note the grim summons. Barnes and Robertson note the call: "an angel standing in the sun; and he cried with a loud voice, saying to all the fowls that fly in the midst of heaven, Come and gather yourselves together unto the supper of the great God" — an angel, standing in the sun (in a place of full visibility and glory), calls all the birds of prey to gather to "the supper of the great God"; the grim, ironic feast — the birds are summoned to feed on the slain enemies of Christ (vv.18, 21; drawn from Ezekiel 39:17-20).

On "an angel standing in the sun," the witnesses note the herald. Barnes and Gill note that the angel "standing in the sun" cries with a loud voice — conspicuous and far-heard; the herald of the coming slaughter.

On "the supper of the great God," the grammatical-historical note the grim feast: "the supper of the great God" — in dreadful contrast to the marriage supper of the Lamb (v.9), this is the supper at which the birds feast on the corpses of the slain; the figure of the total destruction of Christ's enemies; the great God's supper is the carnage of the wicked. The verse displays the angel calling the birds to the great supper of God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "an angel standing in the sun... saying to all the fowls... Come and gather yourselves together unto the supper of the great God," the angel calling the birds of prey to feast on the slain (Ezekiel 39:17, Barnes, Robertson); "an angel standing in the sun" the conspicuous herald (Barnes, Gill); "the supper of the great God," the grim feast on the corpses of the wicked, contrasting the marriage supper (v.9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the grim summons. The verse marks the angel: in the sun. The verse displays the call: to the birds. The verse displays the gathering: to the supper. The verse displays the feast: the great God's. The summons displays the birds called to feast on the slain enemies. The verse displays the angel: crying. The verse displays the birds: summoned. The verse displays the supper: grim. The angel calling the birds to the great supper of God displays the herald of the slaughter: an angel in the sun calls all the birds of prey to gather to the supper of the great God — the grim feast on the corpses of Christ's slain enemies, in dreadful contrast to the marriage supper of the Lamb. The verse displays the called birds, to eat the flesh of the slain (v.18).
18:
"That ye may eat the flesh of kings, and the flesh of captains, and the flesh of mighty men, and the flesh of horses, and of them that sit on them, and the flesh of all men, both free and bond, both small and great." The very brief verse notes the birds feasting on the flesh of the slain. The grammatical voices note the comprehensive carnage. Barnes and Robertson note the summons' content: "that ye may eat the flesh of kings, and the flesh of captains, and the flesh of mighty men, and the flesh of horses, and of them that sit on them, and the flesh of all men, both free and bond, both small and great" — the birds are to eat the flesh of all the slain — kings, captains, mighty men, horses and riders, all men of every rank; the total slaughter of the enemies of Christ, embracing all classes.

On "the flesh of kings, and the flesh of captains, and the flesh of mighty men," the witnesses note the ranks. Barnes and Gill note that the slain include "kings... captains... mighty men... horses... all men, both free and bond, both small and great" — the whole array of the antichristian host, high and low, is destroyed; no rank escapes; the comprehensive ruin of Christ's enemies.

On "both free and bond, both small and great," the grammatical-historical note the universal slaughter: "both free and bond, both small and great" — all classes, without exception, are among the slain; the destruction is total and embraces every degree; the carnage of the great supper spares none of the enemies of Christ. The verse displays the birds feasting on the flesh of the slain.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the carnage — "that ye may eat the flesh of kings, and... captains, and... mighty men, and... horses, and... all men, both free and bond, both small and great," the birds feasting on the slain of every rank (Barnes, Robertson); "kings... captains... mighty men... horses... all men," the whole antichristian host destroyed (Barnes, Gill); "both free and bond, both small and great," the universal, total slaughter (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the feast of carnage. The verse marks the flesh: of kings. The verse displays the slain: captains, mighty men. The verse displays the host: horses and riders. The verse displays the scope: all, small and great. The feast displays the total destruction of Christ's enemies. The verse displays the flesh: eaten. The verse displays the ranks: all. The verse displays the slaughter: universal. The birds feasting on the flesh of the slain display the comprehensive carnage of the great supper: the birds eat the flesh of kings, captains, mighty men, horses and riders, and all men of every rank — the total destruction of the antichristian host, sparing none. The verse displays the slain host, gathered with the beast for battle (v.19).
19:
"And I saw the beast, and the kings of the earth, and their armies, gathered together to make war against him that sat on the horse, and against his army." The brief verse notes the beast and kings gathered to war against Christ. The grammatical voices note the final muster. Barnes and Robertson note the assembling: "I saw the beast, and the kings of the earth, and their armies, gathered together to make war against him that sat on the horse, and against his army" — the beast (the persecuting power), the kings of the earth, and their armies are gathered (as 16:14, 16, at Armageddon) to make war against Christ (the rider) and his heavenly army; the final muster of the antichristian powers for the last battle.

On "the beast, and the kings of the earth, and their armies," the witnesses note the assembled enemies. Barnes and Gill note that "the beast... the kings of the earth, and their armies" — the whole antichristian confederacy (the beast and the ten kings, 17:12-14) assembles; the concentrated might of the enemies of Christ.

On "gathered together to make war against him that sat on the horse," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the futile war: they gather "to make war against him that sat on the horse, and against his army" — the antichristian powers presume to war against Christ and his host; the futile, doomed assault on the King of kings (17:14); the last battle, which ends not in conflict but in the capture and destruction of the beast (v.20). The verse displays the beast and kings gathered to war against Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the muster — "the beast, and the kings of the earth, and their armies, gathered together to make war against him that sat on the horse," the antichristian powers assembled for the last battle (16:14, 16, Barnes, Robertson); "the beast... the kings of the earth, and their armies," the whole confederacy of Christ's enemies (17:12-14, Barnes, Gill); "to make war against him that sat on the horse, and against his army," the futile, doomed assault on Christ (17:14, Cyril). The verse displays the gathered enemies. The verse marks the beast: assembled. The verse displays the kings: and armies. The verse displays the war: against Christ. The verse displays the assault: futile. The muster displays the antichristian powers gathered against the King of kings. The verse displays the beast: mustered. The verse displays the kings: gathered. The verse displays the war: doomed. The beast and kings gathered to war against Christ display the final muster of the enemies: the beast, the kings of the earth, and their armies assemble to make war against the rider and his army — the doomed, futile assault on the King of kings. The verse displays the gathered host, taken and cast into the lake of fire (v.20).
20:
"And the beast was taken, and with him the false prophet that wrought miracles before him, with which he deceived them that had received the mark of the beast, and them that worshipped his image. These both were cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone." The witnesses note the beast and false prophet cast into the lake of fire. On "the beast was taken, and with him the false prophet that wrought miracles before him," the grammatical voices note the capture. Barnes and Robertson note the outcome of the battle: "the beast was taken, and with him the false prophet that wrought miracles before him, with which he deceived them that had received the mark of the beast" — the beast and the false prophet (the second beast, 13:11-17, who deceived the world by his miracles) are captured; the two great antichristian powers are taken without a battle described; their overthrow is decisive and swift.

On "with which he deceived them that had received the mark of the beast, and them that worshipped his image," the witnesses note the false prophet's crime. Barnes and Gill note that the false prophet "deceived them that had received the mark of the beast, and... worshipped his image" — his miracles (13:13-14) deceived the world into beast-worship; his deception is the ground of his judgment.

On "These both were cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone," the grammatical-historical note the doom: "these both were cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone" — the beast and false prophet are cast alive into the lake of fire (the first mention of the lake of fire, 20:10, 14-15); their final, decisive destruction; the two antichristian powers meet their appointed end in the lake of fire. The verse displays the beast and false prophet cast into the lake of fire.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the capture — "the beast was taken, and with him the false prophet that wrought miracles before him," the two antichristian powers captured (13:11-17, Barnes, Robertson); "deceived them that had received the mark of the beast, and... worshipped his image," the false prophet's deception the ground of judgment (13:13-14, Barnes, Gill); "these both were cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone," their final destruction in the lake of fire (20:10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the doomed powers. The verse marks the beast: taken. The verse displays the false prophet: captured. The verse displays the deception: the mark and image. The verse displays the doom: the lake of fire. The judgment displays the antichristian powers cast into the lake of fire. The verse displays the beast: seized. The verse displays the prophet: judged. The verse displays the fire: their end. The beast and false prophet cast into the lake of fire display the decisive overthrow of the antichristian powers: the beast and the false prophet who deceived the world are taken and cast alive into the lake of fire burning with brimstone — their final, appointed destruction. The verse displays the doomed powers, while the rest are slain by the sword (v.21).
21:
"And the remnant were slain with the sword of him that sat upon the horse, which sword proceeded out of his mouth: and all the fowls were filled with their flesh." The witnesses note the rest slain by the sword of Christ's mouth. On "the remnant were slain with the sword of him that sat upon the horse, which sword proceeded out of his mouth," the grammatical voices note the destruction of the host. Barnes and Robertson note the slaughter: "the remnant were slain with the sword of him that sat upon the horse, which sword proceeded out of his mouth" — the rest (the armies of the beast and kings, v.19), after the beast and false prophet are taken, are slain by the sword of Christ — the sword of his mouth (v.15; the word of his judgment); Christ destroys the antichristian host by the word of his power.

On "which sword proceeded out of his mouth," the witnesses note the word of judgment. Barnes and Gill note that the sword "proceeded out of his mouth" (v.15; 1:16) — Christ conquers and slays not by carnal weapons but by the word of his judgment; his spoken word destroys his enemies; the victory is wholly his.

On "all the fowls were filled with their flesh," the grammatical-historical note the fulfilled feast: "all the fowls were filled with their flesh" — the birds summoned to the great supper (vv.17-18) are now filled with the flesh of the slain; the grim feast is accomplished; the total destruction of Christ's enemies is complete, and the birds gorge on the carnage. The verse displays the rest slain by the sword of Christ's mouth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the slaughter — "the remnant were slain with the sword of him that sat upon the horse, which sword proceeded out of his mouth," the host slain by the word of Christ's judgment (v.15, Barnes, Robertson); "which sword proceeded out of his mouth," Christ conquering by his word, not carnal weapons (1:16, Barnes, Gill); "all the fowls were filled with their flesh," the grim feast accomplished, the carnage complete (vv.17-18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the slain host. The verse marks the remnant: slain. The verse displays the sword: of his mouth. The verse displays the victory: by the word. The verse displays the feast: the birds filled. The slaughter displays Christ destroying his enemies by his word. The verse displays the host: slain. The verse displays the sword: from his mouth. The verse displays the fowls: filled. The rest slain by the sword of Christ's mouth displays the completion of the conquest: the remnant of the antichristian host are slain by the sword of Christ's mouth, and all the birds are filled with their flesh — Christ destroying his enemies by the word of his power, the grim feast accomplished. The verse displays the vanquished enemies, closing the vision before the binding of Satan (ch.20).
